text: The psychologist Ulric Neisser proposed in 1976 that perception is not passive reception but an active cycle: we anticipate what we will perceive, direct our senses accordingly, and update our anticipations based on what we find. This 'perceptual cycle' means attention is not a spotlight we shine on the world — it is a hypothesis we hold about what matters. The implications are startling: two people in the same room, sharing the same sensory inputs, can inhabit radically different perceptual worlds simply because they carry different anticipatory schemas. What we call 'reality' is partly a projection of what we expected to see.
question: In Neisser's perceptual cycle, what role do anticipations play?

options.text: They direct the senses and are updated by what we perceive
They suppress irrelevant sensory inputs permanently
They store sensory data for later processing
